item.title: Internet será irrespirable los días de fútbol y otros deportes. Telefónica extiende los bloqueos a Champions, tenis y golf.
Getting the Flock out
item.description: I wrote to Flock’s privacy contact to opt out of their …

item.title: Terminator: The Story of the 6502 Code You See Onscreen
item.description:
What are the origins of the 6502 assembly code that you see onscreen through the Terminator's vision? Dave traces its origins and explains its purpose.
Comments with question marks get answered on our weekly ShopTalk!: https://youtu.be/iT0T52OV9B8
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Thousands of rare concert recordings are landing on the Internet Archive -- listen now | TechCrunch
item.description: Chicago-based music superfan Aadam Jacobs has been recording the concerts he goes to since the 1980s, amassing an archive of over 10,000 tapes.

item.title: What is jj and why should I care?
item.description: jj is the name of the CLI for Jujutsu. Jujutsu is a DVCS, or "distributed version control system." You may be familiar with other DVCSes, such as git, and this tutorial assumes you're coming to jj from git.

item.title: Backblaze has quietly stopped backing up your data
item.description: TLDR: Despite claiming to backup all your data, Backblaze quietly stopped backing up OneDrive and Dropbox folders - along with potentially many other things.

item.title: Back button hijacking is going away
item.description: When websites are blatantly hostile, users close them to never come back. Have you ever downloaded an app, realized it was deceptive, and deleted it immediately? It's a common occurrence for me. But t

item.title: Lean proved this program was correct; then I found a bug.
item.description: I fuzzed a verified implementation of zlib and found a buffer overflow in the Lean runtime.

item.title: Introducing a new spam policy for "back button hijacking" | Google Search Central Blog | Google for Developers
item.description: April 13, 2026

item.title: DaVinci Resolve – Photo | Blackmagic Design
item.description: Professional video editing, color correction, visual effects and audio post production all in a single application. Free and paid versions for Mac, Windows and Linux.

item.title: I learned something about GPUs today
item.description: Debugging a graphics bug in Blackshift

item.title: Building a CLI for all of Cloudflare
item.description: We’re introducing cf, a new unified CLI designed for consistency across the Cloudflare platform, alongside Local Explorer for debugging local data. These tools simplify how developers and AI agents interact with our nearly 3,000 API operations.

item.title: The Solution Space
item.description: I wanted to write this post in markdown, so I did a quick search for a browser-based editor, but quickly questioned why I was doing that. Instead of searching for one that I liked, I popped open codex to build me one. It coded a sufficient version in a couple minutes from the following prompt (typo included):

item.title: Send a Program, Not a Data Structure
item.description: A look at one of the most underappreciated ideas in system design.

item.title: Your intuition of LLM token usage might be wrong
item.description: I just finished a task with GPT-5.4-mini. Here’s the session summary from oh-my-pi (an agent harness):

item.title: Checking my model vibes against SWE-Bench Pro
item.description: I thought GPT models felt slow and token hungry, and Claude models were faster. I was wrong.

item.title: The Command Line That Never Died
item.description: Inside Amadeus cryptic mode: the terminal language designed for teletypes that still backs a huge share of agency and GDS-sourced bookings worldwide.

item.title: GitHub Stacked PRs
item.description: Break large changes into small, reviewable, stacked pull requests with first-class GitHub support.

item.title: The Future of Everything is Lies, I Guess: Safety
item.description: This is a long article, so I'm breaking it up into a series of posts which will be released over the next few days. You can also read the full work as a PDF or EPUB; these files will be updated as each section is released.

item.title: Why I Don't Ship Preferences
item.description: Most software overloads users with settings — but what if that’s the problem? In this article we argue for fewer preferences and stronger defaults, showing how opinionated design reduces complexity, improves usability, and lets software just work.

item.title: Mailing things is Fun
item.description: Sometimes I can't tell if I'm discovering a cool new hobby, or just getting old. Regardless, today I come to you with a tale of a little endeavor of mine.

item.title: Countdown Standard
Stop Writing Code | Blog
item.description: Ryan X. Charles

item.title: Uses for nested promises – The If Works
item.description: A recent conversation on Mastodon reminded me of some old JavaScript history. When promises were relatively new and the Promises/A+ spec was being developed, there was a request from people with backgrounds in functional programming to incorporate monads and category theory. This issue generated a heated debate, the two sides so diametrically opposed that they both believed their position was so obviously correct that they could not fathom why anyone could believe anything else. Historically I have tended to side with the spec authors in opposing this request, but I recently ran into a case where it would have been useful for things to go the other way, which is a good prompt for re-evaluating your positions.

item.title: What Happened After My Vercel Plugin Post Hit #1 on Hacker News
item.description: 13 Apr 2026 What Happened After My Vercel Plugin Post Hit #1 on Hacker News

item.title: 1 year of LLMs writing code for me
item.description: I have been an early adopter of AI coding tools since they became genuinely useful. Here is what that actually looks like day to day, and why experience matters more than ever.

item.title: Why didn’t IPv6 work in my home network?
item.description: Debugging why IPv6 was not working on my home network even though my ISP supports it.

item.title: The Lucy Syndrome: Why LLMs Forget Corrections | Victor Del Puerto
item.description: LLMs don't remember yesterday. That gap has a name, a causal mechanism, and a fix that doesn't require better memory.

item.title: Average Is All You Need
item.description: LLMs will make more of your average stuff. And that's OK. Here's how average SQL beats fancy attribution models when your agent writes the queries.
item.author: (rawquery team)

item.title: On The Simplicity of Humanness
item.description: Just yesterday, Bryan Cantrill published a short piece that touches something that I think most of us fundamentally understand, but don't have the words. The article is very short, titled "The peril of laziness lost". I recommend you all read it. I plucked out one sentence for you:

item.title: Home-cooked software - Ham Vocke
item.description: Software can be a home-cooked meal. I experienced what that could mean first-hand.

item.title: Continuous Collision Detection as a Visual Effect
item.description: Continuous collision detection is useful in physics engines and can also look quite cool.

item.title: Make tmux Pretty and Usable - Ham Vocke
item.description: Customize the look and feel of tmux
